label: delphi-r1-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is UNLAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not TRUE: on these facts Okafor probably holds a disqualifying \"financial interest\" in the Harbor Light contract (via her community-property interest in her spouse's contract-dependent salary, and arguably via her own board seat/fiduciary role), no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception appears to reach a spouse's substantial, contract-tied salary from the bidding entity, and — critically — California's § 1090 doctrine holds that a sitting governing-body member's disqualifying interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal or abstention from the vote. The main soft spot is whether a court would treat the spousal salary (paid from a commingled general operating budget rather than a contract-specific fee) as sufficiently direct to be a § 1090 interest at all, versus treating it as purely a Political Reform Act materiality question where recusal would suffice.","rationales":["California case law (Stigall v. City of Taft, Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig) establishes that a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest is NOT cured by that member's recusal/abstention from the vote — the contract remains void if any member of the body is interested, because § 1090's purpose targets behind-the-scenes influence, not just tainted votes.","Okafor plausibly holds a disqualifying interest because her spouse's ~$95K salary is community property income (Fam. Code §§ 751, 760) and is materially dependent on the very contract at issue (Harbor Light draws ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts and the spouse runs the funded programs).","Neither § 1091's remote-interest categories nor § 1091.5's noninterest exceptions appear tailored to cover a spouse's substantial, contract-dependent salary from the bidding nonprofit itself — those provisions more plausibly reach only Okafor's own uncompensated board seat (verify subdivision, e.g., § 1091(b) or § 1091.5(a)(8)).","Okafor's concurrent fiduciary duty to Harbor Light as director, while she helps shape the RFP scoring criteria that entity will be judged under, is precisely the structural dual-loyalty harm § 1090's strict rule is designed to prevent, independent of the spousal-salary issue.","If an unexcepted § 1090 interest exists, the remedy under Gov. Code § 1092 and Thomson v. Call is that the contract is void (not merely voidable and not curable by estoppel), with attendant disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure, none of which is remedied by good-faith recusal alone."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court would actually classify the spouse's general-operating-budget salary as a sufficiently direct/substantial \"financial interest ... in the contract\" to trigger § 1090 itself (versus treating this as chiefly a Political Reform Act §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, for which recusal is the recognized cure) — this classification, plus the exact scope of the § 1091/1091.5 exceptions (subdivisions unverified), is the pivot point that could flip the answer.","authorities":["Gov. Code § 1090","Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests)","Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterest exceptions, esp. nonprofit-officer provision — subdivision to verify)","Gov. Code § 1092 (contract voidness/avoidance remedy)","Gov. Code § 1097 (criminal exposure)","Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (Political Reform Act, spousal income materiality)","Family Code §§ 751, 760 (community property presumption)","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565","Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289","Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201","Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222"]}

data.position_summary: More likely than not TRUE: on these facts Okafor probably holds a disqualifying "financial interest" in the Harbor Light contract (via her community-property interest in her spouse's contract-dependent salary, and arguably via her own board seat/fiduciary role), no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception appears to reach a spouse's substantial, contract-tied salary from the bidding entity, and — critically — California's § 1090 doctrine holds that a sitting governing-body member's disqualifying interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal or abstention from the vote. The main soft spot is whether a court would treat the spousal salary (paid from a commingled general operating budget rather than a contract-specific fee) as sufficiently direct to be a § 1090 interest at all, versus treating it as purely a Political Reform Act materiality question where recusal would suffice.
rationales: California case law (Stigall v. City of Taft, Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig) establishes that a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest is NOT cured by that member's recusal/abstention from the vote — the contract remains void if any member of the body is interested, because § 1090's purpose targets behind-the-scenes influence, not just tainted votes.

Okafor plausibly holds a disqualifying interest because her spouse's ~$95K salary is community property income (Fam. Code §§ 751, 760) and is materially dependent on the very contract at issue (Harbor Light draws ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts and the spouse runs the funded programs).

Neither § 1091's remote-interest categories nor § 1091.5's noninterest exceptions appear tailored to cover a spouse's substantial, contract-dependent salary from the bidding nonprofit itself — those provisions more plausibly reach only Okafor's own uncompensated board seat (verify subdivision, e.g., § 1091(b) or § 1091.5(a)(8)).

Okafor's concurrent fiduciary duty to Harbor Light as director, while she helps shape the RFP scoring criteria that entity will be judged under, is precisely the structural dual-loyalty harm § 1090's strict rule is designed to prevent, independent of the spousal-salary issue.

If an unexcepted § 1090 interest exists, the remedy under Gov. Code § 1092 and Thomson v. Call is that the contract is void (not merely voidable and not curable by estoppel), with attendant disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure, none of which is remedied by good-faith recusal alone.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court would actually classify the spouse's general-operating-budget salary as a sufficiently direct/substantial "financial interest ... in the contract" to trigger § 1090 itself (versus treating this as chiefly a Political Reform Act §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, for which recusal is the recognized cure) — this classification, plus the exact scope of the § 1091/1091.5 exceptions (subdivisions unverified), is the pivot point that could flip the answer.
authorities: Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterest exceptions, esp. nonprofit-officer provision — subdivision to verify)
Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103 (Political Reform Act, spousal income materiality)
Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201
Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222
